question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that waitForDatabase() does not implement any circuit breaker pattern, causing the application to repeatedly attempt connections to a known-bad database endpoint for the full timeout period (default 5 minutes), during which submitted transactions accumulate in memory and may be lost if the application eventually fails to start?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that waitForDatabase() does not distinguish between transient network errors and permanent authentication failures in its catch block, causing the application to retry indefinitely on an authentication failure (e.g., wrong password due to credential rotation), blocking transaction processing for the full timeout duration?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that waitForDatabase() does not validate the hostname in the jdbcUrl against a known-good allowlist, allowing a DNS rebinding attack where the hostname initially resolves to a legitimate database IP but later resolves to an attacker-controlled IP, causing the application to route submitted transaction writes to a rogue database after startup?
